pages.text:
Behavioral models
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 35

Behavioral models
 Behavioral models are models of the dynamic behavior
of a system as it is executing. They show what happens
or what is supposed to happen when a system responds
to a stimulus from its environment.
 You can think of these stimuli as being of two types:
 Data Some data arrives that has to be processed by the system.
 Events Some event happens that triggers system processing.
Events may have associated data, although this is not always
the case.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 36

Data-driven modeling
 Many business systems are data-processing systems
that are primarily driven by data. They are controlled by
the data input to the system, with relatively little external
event processing.
 Data-driven models show the sequence of actions
involved in processing input data and generating an
associated output.
 They are particularly useful during the analysis of
requirements as they can be used to show end-to-end
processing in a system.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 37

An activity model of the insulin pump’s
operation
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 38

Order processing
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 39

Event-driven modeling
 Real-time systems are often event-driven, with minimal
data processing. For example, a landline phone
switching system responds to events such as ‘receiver
off hook’ by generating a dial tone.
 Event-driven modeling shows how a system responds to
external and internal events.
 It is based on the assumption that a system has a finite
number of states and that events (stimuli) may cause a
transition from one state to another.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 40

State machine models
 These model the behaviour of the system in response to
external and internal events.
 They show the system’s responses to stimuli so are
often used for modelling real-time systems.
 State machine models show system states as nodes and
events as arcs between these nodes. When an event
occurs, the system moves from one state to another.
 Statecharts are an integral part of the UML and are used
to represent state machine models.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 41

State diagram of a microwave oven
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 42

Microwave oven operation
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 43

States and stimuli for the microwave oven (a)
State Description
Waiting The oven is waiting for input. The display shows the current time.
Half power The oven power is set to 300 watts. The display shows ‘Half power’.
Full power The oven power is set to 600 watts. The display shows ‘Full power’.
Set time The cooking time is set to the user’s input value. The display shows
the cooking time selected and is updated as the time is set.
Disabled Oven operation is disabled for safety. Interior oven light is on.
Display shows ‘Not ready’.
Enabled Oven operation is enabled. Interior oven light is off. Display shows
‘Ready to cook’.
Operation Oven in operation. Interior oven light is on. Display shows the timer
countdown. On completion of cooking, the buzzer is sounded for five
seconds. Oven light is on. Display shows ‘Cooking complete’ while
buzzer is sounding.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 44
tables: The oven is waiting for input. The display shows the current time.
The oven power is set to 300 watts. The display shows ‘Half power’.
The oven power is set to 600 watts. The display shows ‘Full power’.

The cooking time is set to the user’s input value. The display shows
the cooking time selected and is updated as the time is set.

Oven operation is disabled for safety. Interior oven light is on.
Display shows ‘Not ready’.

Oven operation is enabled. Interior oven light is off. Display shows
‘Ready to cook’.

Oven in operation. Interior oven light is on. Display shows the timer
countdown. On completion of cooking, the buzzer is sounded for five
seconds. Oven light is on. Display shows ‘Cooking complete’ while
buzzer is sounding.

pages.text:
States and stimuli for the microwave oven (b)
Stimulus Description
Half power The user has pressed the half-power button.
Full power The user has pressed the full-power button.
Timer The user has pressed one of the timer buttons.
Number The user has pressed a numeric key.
Door open The oven door switch is not closed.
Door closed The oven door switch is closed.
Start The user has pressed the Start button.
Cancel The user has pressed the Cancel button.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 45
tables: The user has pressed the half-power button.
The user has pressed the full-power button.
The user has pressed one of the timer buttons.
The user has pressed a numeric key.
The oven door switch is not closed.
The oven door switch is closed.
The user has pressed the Start button.
The user has pressed the Cancel button.

pages.text:
Context models
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 8

Context models
 Context models are used to illustrate the operational
context of a system - they show what lies outside the
system boundaries.
 Social and organisational concerns may affect the
decision on where to position system boundaries.
 Architectural models show the system and its
relationship with other systems.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 9

System boundaries
 System boundaries are established to define what is
inside and what is outside the system.
 They show other systems that are used or depend on the system
being developed.
 The position of the system boundary has a profound
effect on the system requirements.
 Defining a system boundary is a political judgment
 There may be pressures to develop system boundaries that
increase / decrease the influence or workload of different parts of
an organization.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 10

The context of the Mentcare system
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 11

Process perspective
 Context models simply show the other systems in the
environment, not how the system being developed is
used in that environment.
 Process models reveal how the system being developed
is used in broader business processes.
 UML activity diagrams may be used to define business
process models.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 12

Process model of involuntary detention
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 13

Core Principles of Object-Oriented Design
Understanding object-oriented modeling begins with mastering four key
design principles that shape robust software systems:
Abstraction – Focuses on essential attributes and behaviors, hiding
unnecessary detail.
Encapsulation – Bundles data and behavior together and restricts
direct access to internals.
Decomposition – Breaks complex systems into smaller, manageable
parts with clear responsibilities.
Generalization – Identifies shared structure or behavior across
classes and centralizes it to reduce duplication.
These principles work best in combination to create flexible, maintainable
systems.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 19/92
tables:
Understanding object-oriented modeling begins with mastering four key
design principles that shape robust software systems:
Abstraction – Focuses on essential attributes and behaviors, hiding
unnecessary detail.
Encapsulation – Bundles data and behavior together and restricts
direct access to internals.
Decomposition – Breaks complex systems into smaller, manageable
parts with clear responsibilities.
Generalization – Identifies shared structure or behavior across
classes and centralizes it to reduce duplication.
These principles work best in combination to create flexible, maintainable
systems.

pages.text:
Abstraction in Object-Oriented Design
Definition
Abstraction is the idea of simplifying a concept in the problem domain to
its essentials within some context. It allows you to better understand a
concept by breaking it down into a simplified description that ignores
unimportant details.
Helps manage complexity by focusing on only what matters for a
specific purpose.
Eliminates unnecessary attributes or behaviors from the design.
In object-oriented modeling, abstraction is realized through classes.
Different contexts lead to different abstractions of the same concept.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 20/92
tables:
Definition
Abstraction is the idea of simplifying a concept in the problem domain to
its essentials within some context. It allows you to better understand a
concept by breaking it down into a simplified description that ignores
unimportant details.
Helps manage complexity by focusing on only what matters for a
specific purpose.
Eliminates unnecessary attributes or behaviors from the design.
In object-oriented modeling, abstraction is realized through classes.
Different contexts lead to different abstractions of the same concept.

pages.text:
Forming Abstractions Using Context
Rule of Least Astonishment
The abstraction captures the essential attributes and behavior for a
concept with no surprises and no definitions that fall beyond its scope.
You don’t want to surprise anyone trying to understand your abstraction
with irrelevant characteristics.
Abstractions must be formed relative to a specific context or system
goal.
The same concept may have different abstractions in different
applications.
Example: A "Person" abstraction in a driving app vs. a restaurant
app will focus on different characteristics.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 21/92
tables:
Rule of Least Astonishment
The abstraction captures the essential attributes and behavior for a
concept with no surprises and no definitions that fall beyond its scope.
You don’t want to surprise anyone trying to understand your abstraction
with irrelevant characteristics.
Abstractions must be formed relative to a specific context or system
goal.
The same concept may have different abstractions in different
applications.
Example: A "Person" abstraction in a driving app vs. a restaurant
app will focus on different characteristics.

pages.text:
Class and Abstraction
Class is the abstraction template of a person.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 22/92
tables: Class is the abstraction template of a person.

pages.text:
Object and Instantiation
Object instantiated of a class has the essential details for that
instance in that context.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 23/92
tables:
Object instantiated of a class has the essential details for that
instance in that context.

pages.text:
Abstraction Example: Student in Academia
Context: Academic system modeling students.
Relevant attributes:
Courses enrolled
Grades
Student ID
These are stable attributes; values may change, but their presence is
constant.
Relevant behaviors:
Studying
Doing assignments
Attending lectures
Irrelevant attributes in this context:
Pet ownership
Favorite video game
Kitchen cleaning habits
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 24/92

Relevant behaviors:

Irrelevant attributes in this context:

pages.text:
Practice: House Cat Abstraction (Owner Perspective)
Relevant attributes:
Name
Color
Microchip ID
Favorite nap location
Relevant behaviors:
Napping
Grooming
Eating
Using the litter box
Irrelevant details are excluded — e.g., the brand of cat food or room
temperature.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 25/92

Relevant behaviors:

Eating

pages.text:
Why Abstraction Matters
Abstraction simplifies class design by focusing only on what is
essential.
It improves clarity and keeps each class aligned with a specific
purpose.
Well-formed abstractions make the system easier to understand,
maintain, and extend.
They encourage thoughtful decisions about what each class should
represent and do.
Abstractions are not fixed — they evolve as the problem domain and
system requirements change.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 26/92
tables:
Abstraction simplifies class design by focusing only on what is
essential.
It improves clarity and keeps each class aligned with a specific
purpose.
Well-formed abstractions make the system easier to understand,
maintain, and extend.
They encourage thoughtful decisions about what each class should
represent and do.
Abstractions are not fixed — they evolve as the problem domain and
system requirements change.

pages.text:
What is Encapsulation?
Encapsulation is a core object-oriented design principle.
It bundles related data (attributes) and behaviors (methods) into a
single, self-contained unit: the object.
Each class defines the essential structure and behavior of its objects.
Encapsulation makes objects act like "capsules" — some internals are
accessible externally, others are hidden.
Definition
Encapsulation forms a self-contained object by bundling the data and
functions it requires to work, exposes an interface whereby other objects
can access and use it, and restricts access to certain inside details.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 27/92
tables:
Encapsulation is a core object-oriented design principle.
It bundles related data (attributes) and behaviors (methods) into a
single, self-contained unit: the object.
Each class defines the essential structure and behavior of its objects.
Encapsulation makes objects act like "capsules" — some internals are
accessible externally, others are hidden.
Definition
Encapsulation forms a self-contained object by bundling the data and
functions it requires to work, exposes an interface whereby other objects
can access and use it, and restricts access to certain inside details.

pages.text:
Encapsulation Illustrated
Encapsulation hides an object’s internal parts and shows only what’s
needed.
This is how objects work: clean outside, hidden complexity inside.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 28/92
tables:
Encapsulation hides an object’s internal parts and shows only what’s
needed.
This is how objects work: clean outside, hidden complexity inside.

pages.text:
Three Aspects of Encapsulation
Bundling: Group related data and behavior into a single object.
Interface: Expose only selected attributes and methods for use by
other objects.
Hiding: Keep internal details private to protect the object from
unintended access or misuse.
This makes your code easier to understand, modify, and protect.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 29/92
tables:
Bundling: Group related data and behavior into a single object.
Interface: Expose only selected attributes and methods for use by
other objects.
Hiding: Keep internal details private to protect the object from
unintended access or misuse.
This makes your code easier to understand, modify, and protect.

pages.text:
Encapsulation Through Interfaces
Classes define public methods to allow controlled access to data and
behavior.
Example:
A course may offer a method for a student to enroll.
A professor may use a method to retrieve a list of enrolled students.
Internals are protected — attributes are not accessed directly.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 30/92
tables:
Classes define public methods to allow controlled access to data and
behavior.
Example:
A course may offer a method for a student to enroll.
A professor may use a method to retrieve a list of enrolled students.
Internals are protected — attributes are not accessed directly.

pages.text:
Protecting Internal State
Encapsulation restricts direct access to an object’s internal data to
protect integrity.
Instead of accessing fields directly, external code uses public methods
(getters and setters).
This approach allows validation, formatting, or other logic before
data is returned or modified.
Example:
A Student class stores GPA.
It exposes a method getGPA() or better yet, isInGoodStanding().
This keeps GPA access controlled, and prevents unwanted or unsafe
changes.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 31/92
tables:
Encapsulation restricts direct access to an object’s internal data to
protect integrity.
Instead of accessing fields directly, external code uses public methods
(getters and setters).
This approach allows validation, formatting, or other logic before
data is returned or modified.
Example:
A Student class stores GPA.
It exposes a method getGPA() or better yet, isInGoodStanding().
This keeps GPA access controlled, and prevents unwanted or unsafe
changes.

pages.text:
Benefits for Maintenance and Change
Encapsulation hides how data and behavior are implemented behind a
stable interface.
You can change the internal logic without affecting other parts of the
system.
This reduces risk during updates and keeps code modular and
maintainable.
Example:
A professor wants to verify a student’s GPA.
The student might provide it:
From paper records
Through a database
Using an online system
The professor uses the same method call — unaware of the internal
process.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 32/92
tables:
Encapsulation hides how data and behavior are implemented behind a
stable interface.
You can change the internal logic without affecting other parts of the
system.
This reduces risk during updates and keeps code modular and
maintainable.
Example:
A professor wants to verify a student’s GPA.
The student might provide it:
From paper records
Through a database
Using an online system
The professor uses the same method call — unaware of the internal
process.

pages.text:
Black Box Thinking and the Abstraction Barrier
A class should behave like a black box — its internal implementation
remains hidden.
Interaction occurs through public methods that return results or
perform actions.
This abstraction barrier simplifies usage and reduces dependency on
internal details.
Promotes reusability and minimizes the impact of internal changes.
Example
A User class may offer a method getFullName(). The caller does not
need to know whether the name is stored as one field or composed from
first and last names — only that the full name is returned.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 33/92
tables: Black Box Thinking and the Abstraction Barrier

A class should behave like a black box — its internal implementation
remains hidden.
Interaction occurs through public methods that return results or
perform actions.
This abstraction barrier simplifies usage and reduces dependency on
internal details.
Promotes reusability and minimizes the impact of internal changes.
Example
A User class may offer a method getFullName(). The caller does not
need to know whether the name is stored as one field or composed from
first and last names — only that the full name is returned.

pages.text:
Summary: Why Encapsulation Matters
Encapsulation keeps software modular by organizing data and
behavior into self-contained classes.
It improves maintainability by allowing internal implementations to
change without affecting external code.
It enhances security and integrity by preventing direct access to
internal state, reducing the risk of unintended side effects.
It supports black-box thinking — external parts of the system
interact only through well-defined interfaces.
This separation of concerns leads to clearer, more reliable, and
easier-to-manage software.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 34/92
tables:
Encapsulation keeps software modular by organizing data and
behavior into self-contained classes.
It improves maintainability by allowing internal implementations to
change without affecting external code.
It enhances security and integrity by preventing direct access to
internal state, reducing the risk of unintended side effects.
It supports black-box thinking — external parts of the system
interact only through well-defined interfaces.
This separation of concerns leads to clearer, more reliable, and
easier-to-manage software.

pages.text:
What is Decomposition?
Decomposition breaks down a whole into meaningful, manageable
parts.
Alternatively, it can describe assembling parts to form a coherent
whole.
This design principle supports problem solving by simplifying
complexity.
Each part can have its own responsibility, separate from the rest.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 35/92
tables:
Decomposition breaks down a whole into meaningful, manageable
parts.
Alternatively, it can describe assembling parts to form a coherent
whole.
This design principle supports problem solving by simplifying
complexity.
Each part can have its own responsibility, separate from the rest.

pages.text:
Real-World Examples of Decomposition
Example: A car is composed of transmission, motor, wheels, seats,
etc.
Example: A refrigerator includes compressor, freezer, shelves, drawers,
and food.
Each part contributes to the overall goal — e.g., preserving food.
Responsibilities are divided among parts (e.g., freezing delegated to
the freezer).
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 36/92
tables:
Example: A car is composed of transmission, motor, wheels, seats,
etc.
Example: A refrigerator includes compressor, freezer, shelves, drawers,
and food.
Each part contributes to the overall goal — e.g., preserving food.
Responsibilities are divided among parts (e.g., freezing delegated to
the freezer).

pages.text:
Fixed vs. Dynamic Parts
A whole may contain parts in fixed or changing quantities.
Fixed parts stay constant during the object’s lifetime (e.g., one
engine per car).
Dynamic parts may be added or removed over time (e.g., food items
in a fridge).
Example: A car has one steering wheel (fixed), but a variable number
of passengers (dynamic).
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 37/92
tables:
A whole may contain parts in fixed or changing quantities.
Fixed parts stay constant during the object’s lifetime (e.g., one
engine per car).
Dynamic parts may be added or removed over time (e.g., food items
in a fridge).
Example: A car has one steering wheel (fixed), but a variable number
of passengers (dynamic).

pages.text:
Nested Decomposition
Parts can also be wholes with their own subparts.
Example: A refrigerator drawer contains fruit.
Example: A car’s instrument panel contains a speedometer,
odometer, and fuel gauge.
Encapsulation allows these subparts to act as black boxes.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 38/92
tables:
Parts can also be wholes with their own subparts.
Example: A refrigerator drawer contains fruit.
Example: A car’s instrument panel contains a speedometer,
odometer, and fuel gauge.
Encapsulation allows these subparts to act as black boxes.

pages.text:
Lifetime Relationships Between Parts and Wholes
Some parts share the same lifespan as their whole.
Example: Refrigerator and freezer — the freezer is disposed with the
fridge.
Other parts may have independent lifetimes.
Example: Car frame (long-lasting) vs. tires (replaceable).
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 39/92
tables: Lifetime Relationships Between Parts and Wholes

Some parts share the same lifespan as their whole.
Example: Refrigerator and freezer — the freezer is disposed with the
fridge.
Other parts may have independent lifetimes.
Example: Car frame (long-lasting) vs. tires (replaceable).

pages.text:
Shared vs. Exclusive Parts
Parts can be shared among multiple wholes in some designs.
Example: A person might be a spouse in one family and a parent in
another.
Some parts are exclusive and cannot be shared.
Example: A food item cannot be in both a refrigerator and an oven
at once.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 40/92
tables:
Parts can be shared among multiple wholes in some designs.
Example: A person might be a spouse in one family and a parent in
another.
Some parts are exclusive and cannot be shared.
Example: A food item cannot be in both a refrigerator and an oven
at once.

pages.text:
Why Decomposition Matters
Decomposition allows complex systems to be built from simpler,
reusable parts.
Makes responsibilities easier to isolate, understand, and implement.
Supports flexibility in system design (fixed vs. dynamic, shared vs.
exclusive).
Helps organize software architectures for scalability and clarity.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 41/92
tables:
Decomposition allows complex systems to be built from simpler,
reusable parts.
Makes responsibilities easier to isolate, understand, and implement.
Supports flexibility in system design (fixed vs. dynamic, shared vs.
exclusive).
Helps organize software architectures for scalability and clarity.

pages.text:
What is Generalization?
Generalization is a design principle for reducing redundancy in code.
It helps identify and extract common patterns from multiple places.
Allows us to define shared behavior and structure once, and reuse it
in many places.
Generalization applies at both the method and class level in
object-oriented design.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 42/92
tables:
Generalization is a design principle for reducing redundancy in code.
It helps identify and extract common patterns from multiple places.
Allows us to define shared behavior and structure once, and reuse it
in many places.
Generalization applies at both the method and class level in
object-oriented design.

pages.text:
Generalization with Methods
Repeated actions can be extracted into reusable methods.
Instead of duplicating logic, define a method once and call it with
different arguments.
Common in algorithm design where the same logic applies to
different data sets.
This improves readability, reusability, and reduces errors.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 43/92
tables:
Repeated actions can be extracted into reusable methods.
Instead of duplicating logic, define a method once and call it with
different arguments.
Common in algorithm design where the same logic applies to
different data sets.
This improves readability, reusability, and reduces errors.

pages.text:
Generalization with Classes
Shared attributes and behaviors among classes can be factored into a
common superclass.
This superclass provides a base set of features inherited by
subclasses.
Subclasses can extend or override behaviors while inheriting shared
functionality.
Supports reuse, consistency, and flexibility in class design.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 46/92
tables:
Shared attributes and behaviors among classes can be factored into a
common superclass.
This superclass provides a base set of features inherited by
subclasses.
Subclasses can extend or override behaviors while inheriting shared
functionality.
Supports reuse, consistency, and flexibility in class design.

pages.text:
Generalization: Animal Example
Common features can be abstracted into a superclass (e.g., Animal).
Subclasses like Dog and Cat inherit shared attributes and behaviors.
This avoids duplication and supports code reuse.
Animal
legs: int
tail: boolean
walk()
run()
eat()
Dog Cat
breed: string color: string
bark() meow()
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 47/92
tables:
Common features can be abstracted into a superclass (e.g., Animal).
Subclasses like Dog and Cat inherit shared attributes and behaviors.
This avoids duplication and supports code reuse.
Animal
legs: int
tail: boolean
walk()
run()
eat()
Dog Cat
breed: string color: string
bark() meow()

pages.text:
Benefits of Class Generalization
Avoids duplicated code by factoring shared features into one place.
Enables consistent behavior and logic across related classes.
Allows centralized updates: changes in the superclass apply to all
subclasses.
Makes the system easier to extend — new subclasses inherit the
common features automatically.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 48/92
tables:
Avoids duplicated code by factoring shared features into one place.
Enables consistent behavior and logic across related classes.
Allows centralized updates: changes in the superclass apply to all
subclasses.
Makes the system easier to extend — new subclasses inherit the
common features automatically.

pages.text:
D.R.Y. Principle and Generalization
D.R.Y. stands for "Don’t Repeat Yourself".
Generalization supports this by consolidating common code into
shared structures.
Reduces maintenance effort — fewer places to modify when
requirements change.
Leads to code that is more readable, reusable, and less error-prone.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 49/92
tables:
D.R.Y. stands for "Don’t Repeat Yourself".
Generalization supports this by consolidating common code into
shared structures.
Reduces maintenance effort — fewer places to modify when
requirements change.
Leads to code that is more readable, reusable, and less error-prone.

pages.text:
Summary: Why Generalization Matters
Helps identify common patterns across methods and classes.
Reduces code duplication and promotes reuse.
Simplifies updates and future enhancements.
Enables more flexible, scalable, and maintainable system design.
Key technique in building robust object-oriented software.
SanarFawzi (SUE-SED) SoftwareDesignandModeling April1,2026 50/92
tables:
Helps identify common patterns across methods and classes.
Reduces code duplication and promotes reuse.
Simplifies updates and future enhancements.
Enables more flexible, scalable, and maintainable system design.
Key technique in building robust object-oriented software.

pages.text:
Interaction models
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 14

Interaction models
 Modeling user interaction is important as it helps to
identify user requirements.
 Modeling system-to-system interaction highlights the
communication problems that may arise.
 Modeling component interaction helps us understand if a
proposed system structure is likely to deliver the required
system performance and dependability.
 Use case diagrams and sequence diagrams may be
used for interaction modeling.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 15

Use case modeling
 Use cases were developed originally to support
requirements elicitation and now incorporated into the
UML.
 Each use case represents a discrete task that involves
external interaction with a system.
 Actors in a use case may be people or other systems.
 Represented diagramatically to provide an overview of
the use case and in a more detailed textual form.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 16

Transfer-data use case
 A use case in the Mentcare system
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 17

Tabular description of the ‘Transfer data’ use-
case
MHC-PMS: Transfer data
Actors Medical receptionist, patient records system (PRS)
Description A receptionist may transfer data from the Mentcase
system to a general patient record database that is
maintained by a health authority. The information
transferred may either be updated personal information
(address, phone number, etc.) or a summary of the
patient’s diagnosis and treatment.
Data Patient’s personal information, treatment summary
Stimulus User command issued by medical receptionist
Response Confirmation that PRS has been updated
Comments The receptionist must have appropriate security
permissions to access the patient information and the
PRS.

pages.text:
Use cases in the Mentcare system involving the
role ‘Medical Receptionist’
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 19

Sequence diagrams
 Sequence diagrams are part of the UML and are used to
model the interactions between the actors and the
objects within a system.
 A sequence diagram shows the sequence of interactions
that take place during a particular use case or use case
instance.
 The objects and actors involved are listed along the top
of the diagram, with a dotted line drawn vertically from
these.
 Interactions between objects are indicated by annotated
arrows.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 20

Sequence diagram for View patient information
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 21

Sequence
diagram for
Transfer Data
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 22

Structural models
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 23

Structural models
 Structural models of software display the organization of
a system in terms of the components that make up that
system and their relationships.
 Structural models may be static models, which show the
structure of the system design, or dynamic models,
which show the organization of the system when it is
executing.
 You create structural models of a system when you are
discussing and designing the system architecture.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 24

Class diagrams
 Class diagrams are used when developing an object-
oriented system model to show the classes in a system
and the associations between these classes.
 An object class can be thought of as a general definition
of one kind of system object.
 An association is a link between classes that indicates
that there is some relationship between these classes.
 When you are developing models during the early stages
of the software engineering process, objects represent
something in the real world, such as a patient, a
prescription, doctor, etc.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 25

UML classes and association
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 26

Classes and associations in the MHC-PMS
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 27

The Consultation class
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 28

Generalization
 Generalization is an everyday technique that we use to
manage complexity.
 Rather than learn the detailed characteristics of every
entity that we experience, we place these entities in
more general classes (animals, cars, houses, etc.) and
learn the characteristics of these classes.
 This allows us to infer that different members of these
classes have some common characteristics e.g.
squirrels and rats are rodents.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 29

Generalization
 In modeling systems, it is often useful to examine the classes in
a system to see if there is scope for generalization. If changes
are proposed, then you do not have to look at all classes in the
system to see if they are affected by the change.
 In object-oriented languages, such as Java, generalization is
implemented using the class inheritance mechanisms built into
the language.
 In a generalization, the attributes and operations associated with
higher-level classes are also associated with the lower-level
classes.
 The lower-level classes are subclasses inherit the attributes and
operations from their superclasses. These lower-level classes
then add more specific attributes and operations.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 30

A generalization hierarchy
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 31

A generalization hierarchy with added detail
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 32

Object class aggregation models
 An aggregation model shows how classes that are
collections are composed of other classes.
 Aggregation models are similar to the part-of relationship
in semantic data models.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 33

The aggregation association
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 34

Chapter 5 – System Modeling
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 1

Topics covered
 Context models
 Interaction models
 Structural models
 Behavioral models
 Model-driven engineering
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 2

System modeling
 System modeling is the process of developing abstract
models of a system, with each model presenting a
different view or perspective of that system.
 System modeling has now come to mean representing a
system using some kind of graphical notation, which is
now almost always based on notations in the Unified
Modeling Language (UML).
 System modelling helps the analyst to understand the
functionality of the system and models are used to
communicate with customers.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 3

Existing and planned system models
 Models of the existing system are used during requirements
engineering. They help clarify what the existing system does
and can be used as a basis for discussing its strengths and
weaknesses. These then lead to requirements for the new
system.
 Models of the new system are used during requirements
engineering to help explain the proposed requirements to
other system stakeholders. Engineers use these models to
discuss design proposals and to document the system for
implementation.
 In a model-driven engineering process, it is possible to
generate a complete or partial system implementation from
the system model.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 4

System perspectives
 An external perspective, where you model the context or
environment of the system.
 An interaction perspective, where you model the
interactions between a system and its environment, or
between the components of a system.
 A structural perspective, where you model the
organization of a system or the structure of the data that
is processed by the system.
 A behavioral perspective, where you model the dynamic
behavior of the system and how it responds to events.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 5

UML diagram types
 Activity diagrams, which show the activities involved in a
process or in data processing .
 Use case diagrams, which show the interactions
between a system and its environment.
 Sequence diagrams, which show interactions between
actors and the system and between system components.
 Class diagrams, which show the object classes in the
system and the associations between these classes.
 State diagrams, which show how the system reacts to
internal and external events.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 6

Use of graphical models
 As a means of facilitating discussion about an existing or
proposed system
 Incomplete and incorrect models are OK as their role is to
support discussion.
 As a way of documenting an existing system
 Models should be an accurate representation of the system but
need not be complete.
 As a detailed system description that can be used to
generate a system implementation
 Models have to be both correct and complete.
30/10/2014 Chapter 5 System Modeling 7

Mentcare Modeling Overview
Use cases, sequence diagrams, and class diagrams for key receptionist workflows.

pages.text:
MHC-PMS: Register patient
This activity focuses on the initial entry of a patient into the system boundary.
Field Description
Actors Medical receptionist, Mentcare System
The receptionist creates a new digital record for a patient who has not
Description
previously been treated within the system.
Data Full name, date of birth, contact details, and next of kin information.
Stimulus Receptionist selects "New Patient" command.
Response A unique patient identifier is generated and the record is saved.
Verification of identity (e.g., government ID) is typically required before
Comments
registration.
tables:
The receptionist creates a new digital record for a patient who has not
previously been treated within the system.
Full name, date of birth, contact details, and next of kin information.
A unique patient identifier is generated and the record is saved.

Verification of identity (e.g., government ID) is typically required before
registration.

pages.text:
MHC-PMS: Unregister patient
This activity removes or archives a patient from the active system.
Field Description
Actors Medical receptionist, Mentcare System
A patient is removed from the active system roster, typically due to
Description
discharge, death, or transfer to another authority.
Data Patient ID and reason for unregistration.
Stimulus Receptionist selects "Unregister" command for a specific record.
System confirms record status has been updated to 'Inactive' or
Response
'Archived'.
Medical records are rarely deleted entirely; they are usually moved to a
Comments
long-term archive for legal compliance.

patient data.

pages.text:
Sample 1: Internship Matching Portal (University ↔ Companies)
Scenario
Your department wants a portal where companies post internship openings and students apply.
Coordinators need to approve postings, track applications, and export lists for reporting. Students
want to filter by city, domain, and required skills.
Transition (what we extract first)
Actors: Student, Company HR, Department Coordinator
Goal: publish opportunities → apply → review → decide
Key constraints: approval workflow + searchable catalogue
User stories (examples)
 As a Company, I can create an internship post and submit it for approval.
 As a Coordinator, I can approve/reject posts and see applicants.
 As a Student, I can search/filter internships and apply with a CV.
Tasks (Epic: Internship Posts & Applications)
1. Define data model (Company, InternshipPost, SkillTag, Application, Status)
o Done: ERD + field list + statuses agreed (Draft/Submitted/Approved/Rejected/Closed).
2. Create Coordinator approval flow
o Done: company posts land in “Pending”; coordinator can approve/reject with reason.
3. Build Internship listing page (public for students)
o Done: list shows title/company/city/type; loads fast; pagination.
4. Add filters + search (city, domain, skills, keyword)
o Done: filters combine correctly; URL keeps query params.
5. Internship detail page
o Done: full description, requirements, deadline, “Apply” button.
6. Student application form (CV upload + notes)
o Done: validates file types/size; stores safely; application gets status “Submitted”.
7. Company dashboard (see their posts + applicants)
o Done: company can view applicant list and each applicant profile.
8. Coordinator reporting export (CSV/Excel)
o Done: export includes post + applicants + statuses.
9. Notifications (email or in-app)
o Done: notify company on approval; notify student on status changes.
10. Testing & acceptance
 Done: test cases for approval, filtering, applying, permissions.

Sample 2: Clinic Appointment Scheduling (with reminders)
Scenario
A clinic receives too many calls. Patients should book appointments online, choose a doctor, and
receive reminders. Receptionists should approve reschedules and handle cancellations.
Transition
Actors: Patient, Receptionist, Doctor
Goal: book → confirm → remind → handle cancellations
Risk areas: double booking, time slots, reminders
User stories
 As a Patient, I can see available slots and book one.
 As a Receptionist, I can approve reschedule requests.
 As a Doctor, I can see my daily schedule.
Tasks (Epic: Appointment Booking)
1. Model scheduling rules (doctors, working hours, slot length, holidays)
o Done: rules documented + stored in DB.
2. Availability API (return free slots, prevent overlaps)
o Done: no double booking under concurrent requests.
3. Patient booking UI (choose doctor/date/slot)
o Done: booking confirmation screen + error handling.
4. Appointment confirmation workflow
o Done: status = Pending → Confirmed; receptionist can confirm automatically/manual.
5. Reschedule/cancel flow
o Done: patient can request; receptionist approves; audit log kept.
6. Doctor schedule view (day/week)
o Done: shows patient name (or anonymized), reason, time.
7. Reminder system (24h + 2h before)
o Done: reminders sent; failures logged; opt-out supported.
8. Permissions & privacy
o Done: role-based access; no patient data leakage.
9. QA scenarios (double booking, cancellation after reminder, no-show)
o Done: test checklist + fixes.

Sample 3: Lab Equipment Booking + Inventory (University labs)
Scenario
Labs have limited equipment (oscilloscopes, microcontroller kits). Students want to reserve
equipment for time slots. Technicians want to track working/out-of-order units and prevent bookings
for broken items.
Transition
Actors: Student, Lab Technician, Department Admin
Goal: reserve → pick up/return → track condition
Important: inventory status drives booking availability
User stories
 As a Student, I can reserve equipment for a time slot.
 As a Technician, I can mark units as out-of-order.
 As an Admin, I can see usage statistics.
Tasks (Epic: Inventory-aware Booking)
1. Define inventory structure (EquipmentType vs Unit/Serial)
o Done: supports multiple units per equipment type.
2. Booking rules (max hours, conflict checks, late return rules)
o Done: constraints documented + enforced.
3. Search & browse equipment
o Done: filter by lab, category, availability.
4. Booking flow UI (select item → time slot → confirm)
o Done: confirmation + booking record created.
5. Technician dashboard (check-out/check-in)
o Done: changes status (Reserved → CheckedOut → Returned).
6. Maintenance workflow
o Done: technician can mark unit OutOfOrder; unit removed from availability.
7. Notifications (pickup time, overdue alerts)
o Done: alerts trigger on time conditions.
8. Usage reports (most used items, downtime, demand peaks)
o Done: exportable stats.
9. Testing (conflicts, out-of-order unit booked, overdue)
o Done: key edge cases covered.

Sample 4: Event Registration + QR Check-in
Scenario
Your university hosts seminars. People register online, get a QR code, and sta(cid:431) scan it at the entrance
to check attendance. Organizers want attendance stats.
Transition
Actors: Attendee, Organizer, Check-in Sta(cid:431)
Goal: register → generate ticket → scan → report
Risks: duplicate QR use, o(cid:431)line scanning
User stories
 As an Attendee, I can register and receive a QR ticket.
 As Sta(cid:431), I can scan QR and mark attendance.
 As an Organizer, I can see attendance stats.
Tasks (Epic: Registration & Check-in)
1. Registration form + validation
o Done: captures name/email/phone; prevents duplicates by email.
2. QR generation (unique token per registration)
o Done: QR encodes secure token; cannot be guessed easily.
3. Email delivery (QR + event info)
o Done: retries + logs failures.
4. Check-in scanning page/app
o Done: scan → show name/status → mark checked-in.
5. Prevent double check-in
o Done: second scan shows “Already checked in” + time.
6. O(cid:431)line mode (optional) (cache list + sync later)
o Done: works without internet; sync resolves conflicts.
7. Organizer dashboard (registered vs attended)
o Done: live counts + list export.
8. Security & privacy
o Done: sta(cid:431) only sees minimal info; organizer access controlled.
9. QA (lost email, duplicate registration, QR screenshot reuse)
o Done: test scenarios written + verified.
